author: @icebluscorpion
text: People say it's Galvanic reaction because you have copper and Graphene that could get galvanic reaction but they don't think it through because you have both electedes copper and if there would be a galvanic voltage between the Graphene and the copper it would it would be constantly there. But you get only voltage while moving. So that's not galvanic voltage there. I got similar results with two different lenthed strips of aluminum tape and moving tap water even the screws washers and nut were made of aluminum and I got a reading of a couple mV. This aluminum thermal cell is inspired by your work on semiconductor cupper oxide rocket stove generator because you mentioned that it has to have flowing ions to get a higher voltage. So I thought this volte difference induced bei a difference in length of the metal while ions are flowing by could be replicated in water with a high ion content too. Imagine doing that with Graphene

author: @rnicoletaro
text:
Hello,
If I can make a suggestion on that phenomenon: it's related to the zeta potential and what is measured with the voltmeter is called streaming potential. This is generated by moving charged particles through a liquid or by moving the liquid through an immobilized and charged solid surface.
Thanks,
An observer.

author: @Lightslinger42
text: Rob, I've seen an awful lot of your vids mate, and I have an idea that I haven't seen ya try yet...  So, the Graphene creates a really huge area on the surface of the electrode microscopically, right?  I was thinking about how, in order to do this, it must have just an obscene amount of hills and valleys right?  Well, it occurred to me that at the high and low points of each of these hills and valleys there is a "curve" so to speak.  Like a coil turn...  With all the Graphene stuff there is on You Tube to date, I haven't seen anyone try passing a powerful magnet over an electrode like this to see if it might generate power like a coil might...  Have you (or anyone for that matter) tried this?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i try lots of stuff, mate - some of it i show some i don't - the channel is about inspiring you to try - so make some GO and try yourself - see what happens - because it is an interesting question - and having you ask the question is what the channel is about

author: @slackerbelmont
text: I have a question about where you are attaching your leads. You said that the copper is insulated from the graphene, but when you place an alligator clip on the top doesn't it bridge the two?. I guess what I am asking is where does the connection need to be read from the graphene or the copper strips? would wire soldered to the copper suffice?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: the copper strips acts as current collection - they are insulated from each other and insulated from the surrounding environment but connected to the graphene if they weren't it wouldn't work. The arrangement is exactly the same as in a capacitor and one way of looking at this is as a single sided supercapacitor

Which "sea" are you getting the sea water from, and is it actually at sea level?  The Caspian, Salton, and Dead are below sea level.  The Ariel (still disappearing), Azov, and Black are above "sea level", so what is sea level relative too?
Yes, a little off topic, but where else would such a tangent fit?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: indeed

author: @BeefHardslab
text: Is there not a 3rd possibility, that thermal fluctuations can induce a current? Not saying the other 2 don't exist of course, I just seem to remember a graphene interface wherein thermal variation can generate current. Perhaps if you took this same sheet and instead of dousing it in saltwater you clamped your hands upon it, would that generate a reading?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: there is but that, in this context, is still ion impaction. The thermal effect you mention is from the anisotropic nature of functionalised graphene where electron trasport is faster than thermal transport in one direction and the reverse is true at 90 degree to that direction. To take advantage of this you would need to restructure the ink

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: something else i meant to mention - ion impaction works on ambient temperature where temperature is the same throughout whereas the thermal effect you mention works on temperature difference across the sheet

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: another thing I meant to mention this is only as far as I know

replies.author: @BeefHardslab
replies.text: Ok so just theoretical here, but you take your ink, paint it on some plastic one way, then take another sheet of plastic (or maybe it has to be something else?) & paint it again at that 90 degree angle to the first set... would something like that allow a thermal variance current generation?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i did a quick video for you at Thermo Electric Generator From Graphene Ink
